context.mughal: Newly completed under Shah Jahan, white marble still cool to touch. Scaffolding gone for less than a decade. The riverfront garden floods on schedule with Yamuna water.
context.colonial: British engineers measure the dome's curvature with theodolites. Workmen restore inlay stones loosened by neglect. The garden runs dry; lawns replace pools.
context.now: Crowds queue at Diana's Bench. UV-scrubbing keeps the marble from yellowing in modern air.

context.medieval: Capital of Vijayanagara at its zenith. Bazaars stretch along the Tungabhadra. Stone elephants stand in their stables. The Stone Chariot pulls toward the sanctum.
context.mughal: Sacked in 1565 — Deccan sultans burn the city for six months. Boulders cover what bazaars used to be. Pilgrims still come to Virupaksha temple.
context.colonial: Archaeological Survey clears jungle. Drawings of "the lost city" circulate in London. Stone Chariot becomes a postcard.
context.now: UNESCO World Heritage site. Backpacker cafés on the river. Boulders still hold the city's footprint.

context.ancient: First Buddhist monks chisel into the volcanic cliff. Paintings begin in earnest.
context.classical: Vakataka kings finance the great mural cycle. Cave 1, 2, 16, 17 — the masterpieces are wet plaster.
context.colonial: A British hunting party stumbles on the caves in 1819. Smoke from torches blackens the frescoes. Tracings ship to London.
context.now: Climate-controlled lighting. Visitors in shoe covers. Murals preserved one inch at a time.

context.medieval: Built by King Narasimhadeva I as a chariot of the Sun. Twenty-four stone wheels, seven horses, every surface carved. The lodestone-capped tower still stands.
context.colonial: The main shikhara has collapsed. British engineers fill the sealed jagamohana with sand to stop further collapse. "Black Pagoda" on shipping charts.
context.now: Sand removed only partially. Erosion races restoration. The chariot wheels still turn for sunset photographs.

context.mughal: Akbar's capital — built in fourteen years, abandoned in fourteen more. Diwan-i-Khas debates burn through the night. Buland Darwaza, the largest gateway in Asia, frames the sky.
context.colonial: Empty courts. Travel writers describe "a city of dreams". Restoration begins under Curzon.
context.now: School groups in the Panch Mahal. Pigeons in the Jami Masjid courtyards.

context.medieval: Qutbuddin Aibak begins the minaret. Iltutmish finishes the top stages. Quranic calligraphy spirals upward, fresh-cut.
context.colonial: Damaged by earthquakes. Major Smith adds a cupola in 1828 — and is later asked to remove it. Drawings flood Victorian guidebooks.
context.now: Closed to climbers since 1981. Iron pillar still rust-free in the courtyard.

context.medieval: Chandela dynasty at peak. Eighty-five temples in granite and sandstone, every surface alive with figures. Tantric texts in stone.
context.colonial: T. S. Burt rediscovers in 1838 — many temples reclaimed by jungle. Twenty survive.
context.now: UNESCO. Dance festival every February below the Lakshmana temple.

context.medieval: Rao Jodha founds the fort on the bhakurcheeria — the mountain of birds. The cliff is freshly cut to height.
context.mughal: Rajput courts negotiate with Mughal envoys at Phool Mahal. Cannons on Loha Pol.
context.colonial: Princely state under British paramountcy. Royal motorcars climb the new ramp.
context.now: Museum and concert venue. Sandstone glows orange against the blue city below.

context.classical: Buddhist viharas carved from basalt. Monks meditate as the rock is hollowed.
context.medieval: Kailasa Temple begins — top down. Rashtrakuta workmen carve a Himalayan peak into a single stone over 100 years.
context.colonial: Visited by orientalists; etchings appear in the Asiatic Society journals.
context.now: UNESCO. Jain, Hindu, Buddhist caves side by side — a single hillside of devotion.

context.mughal: Shah Jahan's new capital. Diwan-i-Aam holds court. The Peacock Throne is the centerpiece. Yamuna laps at the eastern wall.
context.colonial: British East India Company drains the moat. 1857 ends Mughal rule here; the fort becomes a barracks. White paint covers marble inlay.
context.now: Independence Day flag hoists from the ramparts. Sound and light shows after dark.

context.medieval: Kachhwaha rajas govern from Amer. The fort blends Hindu and emerging Mughal styles.
context.mughal: Sheesh Mahal glittering under candle flame. Elephant processions climb to the gate.
context.colonial: Royal capital moved to Jaipur city. Fort becomes ceremonial.
context.now: Daily elephant rides paused for welfare. Light show after sundown.

context.classical: Pallava chisellers carve granite by the Bay of Bengal. Rathas rise as single boulders sculpted to temples.
context.colonial: British surveyors catalog "the Seven Pagodas". Locals describe sister temples lost to the sea.
context.now: UNESCO. Tsunami of 2004 briefly uncovered submerged structures offshore.
